rationale: The citation to Chernev and Carpenter (2001) is used in the General Discussion to anchor the paper's conceptual positioning on compensatory inferences, not just as a background list item. The authors explicitly compare their findings to prior compensatory-inference work, noting that prior work examined compensatory inferences under same-price/quality conditions, whereas their framework examines how product type shifts lay-theory accessibility and downstream quality judgments/choices. This gives the cited paper a clear framing role in articulating what is being extended in the new contribution.
relation_description: The citing paper extends prior compensatory-inference research represented by Chernev and Carpenter (2001) by applying the idea to company-size-based quality inferences and showing that low-tech vs. high-tech perceptions shift which compensatory lay theory drives judgments and choice.
